coredata.dc:title: Facile and low-energy-consumption synthesis of dual-functional carbon dots from Cornus walteri leaves for detection of p-nitrophenol and photocatalytic degradation of dyes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Congling
Xu, Jun
Zhang, Ruili
Zhao, Weilin
coredata.dc:description: An attempt for sustainable preparation of green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) is made from Cornus walteri leaves through low-temperature hydrothermal treatment. This synthetic approach is low cost, environmental friendliness, and renewability. Significantly, the as-fabricated G-CDs show outstanding fluorescence properties, and the quantum yield (QY) is as high as 18.3%. The G-CDs exhibit a rapid, selective and sensitive response to p-NP, which makes them promising for p-NP sensing. The linearity is in the range of 0–50 μM, and the LOD is as low as 0.0175 μM. Following careful investigations, static quenching was used to explain the fluorescence mechanism. In addition, G-CDs can be regarded as a fast and efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of organic dyes malachite green (MG), methyl orange (MO), and methyl violet (MV). Under the irradiation conditions of 40 min, 50 min, and 90 min, the degradation rates can reach ~98.0%, ~97.1%,and ~63.6%, respectively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bifunctional carbon dots
Cornus walteri
P-nitrophenol detection
Dyes degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of p-NP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of G-CDs in (a) 50 nm, (b) 20 nm, (c)10 nm and (d) 5 nm. (e) Diameter distributions (Curves: Gauss fit) and (f) lattice size of G-CDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the synthesized G-CDs.
High resolution (a) full‐survey, (b) C 1s, (c) O 1s and (d) N 1s XPS spectra of G-CDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra (inset: photograph under ambient light and 365 nm UV light),(b) optimum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs, (c)emission spectra of G-CDs at varying excitation wavelength and (d) decay curve of the G-CDs.

Fluorescence quenching efficiency of (a) aromatic compounds and (b) metal ions to G-CDs. (c) Effect of 0–50 μM p-NP concentration on the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs and (d) Linear relationship between 1/(F0/F) and 1/C(p-NP).

(a) The relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs/p-NP system at different temperatures. (b) The UV–vis absorption spectra of G-CDs and G-CDs/p-NP. (c) The PL decay curve of G-CDs in the absence and presence of p-NP (C(p-NP) = 50 μM). (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of p-NP, and excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs.
Mechanism diagram of p-NP quenching the fluorescence of G-CDs.
Photocatalytic degradation rates of (a) MO, (b) MG and (c) MV by the G-CDs catalyst.(d) Fitting curve for pseudo-first-order degradation kinetics of the G-CDs catalyst.
Scavenger experiments of different radicals during the photocatalytic degradation of MO, MG and MV dyes over G-CDs under visible light irradiation.
Illustration of the possible degradation mechanism.
Synthetic route of G-CDs and process of the fluorescence sensor for detecting p-NP and photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes.
Comparison of LOD with reported p-NP determination.

row.entry: Cornus walteri leaves, maleic anhydride, H2O2

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Determination results of p-NP in actual water (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Facile and low-energy-consumption synthesis of dual-functional carbon dots from Cornus walteri leaves for detection of p-nitrophenol and photocatalytic degradation of dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shandong Provincial Key Laboratory of Preparation and Measurement of Building Materials, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An attempt for sustainable preparation of green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) is made from Cornus walteri leaves through low-temperature hydrothermal treatment. This synthetic approach is low cost, environmental friendliness, and renewability. Significantly, the as-fabricated G-CDs show outstanding fluorescence properties, and the quantum yield (QY) is as high as 18.3%. The G-CDs exhibit a rapid, selective and sensitive response to p-NP, which makes them promising for p-NP sensing. The linearity is in the range of 0–50 μM, and the LOD is as low as 0.0175 μM. Following careful investigations, static quenching was used to explain the fluorescence mechanism. In addition, G-CDs can be regarded as a fast and efficient photocatalyst for the degradation of organic dyes malachite green (MG), methyl orange (MO), and methyl violet (MV). Under the irradiation conditions of 40 min, 50 min, and 90 min, the degradation rates can reach ~98.0%, ~97.1%,and ~63.6%, respectively.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution poses significant challenges to human society with the advance of industrialization, especially water pollution [1,2]. The continuous release of phenol-containing and organic dyes wastewater are major source of water pollutionin [3]. As a typical compound in phenolic wastewater, p-nitrophenol (p-NP) is an acid-base indicator, a leather preservative, and an intermediate of fine chemicals from medicine, dye and pesticide. It has a strong stimulating effect on skin and respiratory tract, and it can cause poisoning when accumulated in the body [4,5]. Therefore, p-NP is designated as a toxic pollutant by the United Nations and the International Maritime Organization [6]. In addition, organic dyes as persistent pollutants, such as malachite green (MG), methyl violet (MV), and methyl orange (MO), can arouse serious problems on environment and health due to their difficulty in biodegradation [7]. MG is a toxic triphenylmethane chemical and its overuse in aquaculture may lead to potential carcinogenic, teratogenic and mutagenic effects. MV is a moderate toxic dye that can induce a variety of body inflammation in people who have long-term contact, resulting in headache, nausea and vomiting. MO is a microtoxic dye, irritating to eyes and sensitizing to skin. Based on the demand of human health and environmental protection, it is urgent to develop economic and effective wastewater treatment technology.

Carbon dots (CDs) due to their good biocompatibility, unique photo-induced electron transfer and low toxicity have drawn more and more attention, and been applied in wastewater treatment [8–11]. CDs are excellent electron donor and electron acceptor materials that can achieve sensitive and rapid detection for nitrophenol compounds with strong electron-withdrawing properties [12]. And solar driven CDs as a green environmental protection technology can realize the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes [13,14].

At present, there are many reports on the preparation of CDs by hydrothermal method [15–17], but issues such as environmental pollution, high synthesis temperatures and complex process plague this approach. For example, Qu et al. [18] have synthesized CDs using ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) as a raw material (160 °C for 6 h) for the detection of p-NP. Rani et al. [19] have applied NCDs synthesized by NaOH oxidation (150 °C for 2 h), immersion (12 h) and hydrothermal (180 °C for 8 h) processes to degrade the methylene blue and malachite green. Therefore, the main challenge is to reduce energy-consumption and simplify process of the CDs synthesis and lower environmental pollution so that they were more effective for the industrial applications.

In this work, Cornus walteri leaves as green biomass material are applied to synthesis water-soluble green fluorescence emission CDs (G-CDs) to lower environmental pollution. Cornus walteri leaves have high yield, and contain abundant free radicals such as polyphenols, flavonoids hydroxyl, and alkyl. A new and simple method for G-CDs synthesis was developed, that is, H2O2 oxidation and hydrothermal treatment (75 °C 1 h), which reduced energy consumption and simplified process. The structure and optical properties of G-CDs from Cornus walteri leaves were systematically studied. The prepared G-CDs play dual-functional roles, one as a fluorescence sensor for selective and sensitive detection of p-NP, and the other as a photocatalyst for rapid and efficient degradation of organic dyes such as MO, MG, and MV. Our objective is to synthesize G-CDs by treating Cornus walteri leaves in a low-energy-consumption method and investigate their ability for detection of p-nitrophenol and photocatalytic degradation of dyes.

Cornus walteri leaves were taken from wild autumn leaves at 36°36' latitude and 116°57' longitude. The chemical maleic anhydride and hydrogen peroxide were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Phenol (P), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), 2,4-dinitrophenol (2,4-DNP), 4-chlorophenol (4-CP), 2,4-dichlorophenol (2,4-DCP), 2,4,6-trichlorophenol (2,4,6-TCP), o-aminophenol (OAP), m-aminophenol (MAP) and p-aminophenol (PAP) were all purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Metal salts (AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, CaCl2, CuSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, KCl, LiNO3, MgCl2, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2 BaCl2, and CrCl6) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (China) Chemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The organic dyes methyl orange (MO), malachite green (MG) and methyl violet (MV) were obtained from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). All reagents are analytical grade and can be used without further purification. Qualitative filter paper with a maximum pore size of 15–20 µm was purchased from Hangzhou Fuyang North Wood Pulp Paper Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). The experimental water was ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm).

A low-temperature hydrothermal process was combined with H2O2 oxidation to successfully synthesize G-CDs from Cornus walteri leaves as a precursor. Specifically, 0.3 g of the dry and fine leaves powder was added into a 50 ml PTFE lining containing 0.1 g maleic anhydride, 10 ml H2O2 and 20 ml water. The resulting solution was kept ultrasonically vibrated at room temperature for 30 min to make uniformly mixed, and then put into the reactor, followed by heating at 75 ℃ for 1 h in the oven. After naturally cooling to 25 ± 2 °C, the sample was centrifuged with 8000 revolutions per minute for 10 min to take away agglomerated precipitates. Then, the collected supernatant was filtered by a filter paper (0.22 µm) to eliminate biggish insoluble. The resulting liquid was dialyzed through a 1000 Da dialysis bag for 24 h to dislodge tiny particles for additional purification. After the above, a pure solution of green-emitting CDs under ultraviolet light irradiation was obtained, referred to as G-CDs. After freeze-drying for 24 h, approximately 0.1 g of the G-CDs powder was procured. In the process of hydrothermal preparation, Cornus walteri leaves as biomass material usually goes through four stages: decomposition, polymerization, carbonization and passivation to form CDs. [20,21].

The selectivity of G-CDs to benzene compounds and metal ions was considered. Benzene compounds include P, p-NP, 2,4-DNP, 4-CP, 2,4-DCP, 2,4,6-TCP, OAP, MAP and PAP. Metal ions include AgNO3, Al(NO3)3, CaCl2, CuSO4, FeCl2, FeCl3, KCl, LiNO3, MgCl2, NaCl, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, BaCl2, and CrCl6. The way, 1 mg of G-CDs catalyst was added into 10 ml of 50 μM benzene compounds and metal ions solutions, severally. After reacting for 3 min, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture were determined at the 505 nm under excitation (420 nm). As a result, the fluorescence intensity of G-CDs was extremely lowered by the existence of p-NP, displaying a high selectivity. For the sensitivity, 1 mg of G-CDs catalyst was dispersed in 10 ml of 0–50μMp-NP aqueous solution. According to the preliminary experiment, we found that the fluorescence intensity had a good linear relationship with the concentration of p-NP.

To verify the reliability of the assay, the real water samples of Jiazi lake and local tap water were collected in University of Jinan (Jinan, China). For further quantitative analysis, 0.22 µm membrane was used to remove impurities after centrifugation at 1800 rpm for 10 min. After that, different concentrations of p-NP (10, 20, and 30 μM) were prepared in 100 ml actual water, followed by mixing with 1 mg G-CDs powder. Subsequently, the fluorescence emission spectra excited at 420 nm were recorded.

G-CDs photocatalyst was tested for the photocatalytic property to degrade the noxious MO, MG and MV dyes under dark and daylight illumination conditions. The specific process was to add 1 mg G-CDs catalyst to 30 ml 30 mg/L model pollutant aqueous solutions. The mixed solutions obtained were stirred in the dark for 20 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. In the following step, a 3 ml sample was drawn for testing every 10 min after the samples were exposed to direct sunlight, to determine the degradation efficiency.

The sunlight illumination condition was located in University of Jinan, China (coordinate: 36°36'47"N 116°57'18"E). In early June, the ambient temperature was between 30 ℃ and 35 ℃ from 10 AM to 2 PM. The change in concentration of dyes was measured by a Metash UV-5800 spectrophotometer. In Scheme 1, the synthetic route of G-CDs and process of the fluorescence sensor for detecting p-NP and photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes are exhibited.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed on Rigaku Ultima IV Advance X-ray Diffractometer using Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation in the 10–60° region at a scanning rate of 4°/min. The morphology and lattice spacing images of G-CDs were accessed by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and Digital Micrograph software, respectively. A Fourier transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) measured by Thermo Nicolet 380 infrared spectrometer between 4000 and 400 cm−1 was used to characterize the surface modification groups. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo ESCALAB XI+ X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra of 300–600 nm were conducted on a Hitachi U-4100 spectrophotometer. On a Hitachi F-4600 fluorescence spectrometer, photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired. The luminescence lifetime measurement was recorded by employing a Hitachi F-4500 lifetime spectrometer equipped with a Horiba tbx picosecond photon detection module. Besides, 1 μg/ml quinine sulfate solution (dissolved in 0.1 mol/L H2SO4) was chosen as the reference solution, the light absorption and fluorescence intensity of the solution at 360 nm were measured, and the fluorescence quantum yield of G-CDs was calculated by the following Eq. (1):
(1)
Q = Q
s
∫
I
∫
I
s
A
s
A
η
2
η
s
2
where Q is quantum yield (QS = 0.54),
∫
I
is integral area of emission peak, Ais UV absorption, η represent refractive index (both are 1.33), subscript S represents the reference solution. In order to reduce the error, the UV absorption at 360 nm should be less than 0.05.

In order to understand the structural, morphological and chemical characterization of G-CDs, XRD, TEM, FT-IR and XPS were measured. The result of XRD spectrum of the synthesized G-CDs is shown in Fig. 1. The XRD pattern of G-CDs shows a sharp characteristic peak near 2θ = 23.3°, indicating the formation of crystalline structures of G-CDs [22]. On the basis of Bragg equation, the G-CDs interlayer spacing (d) is calculated to be 0.38 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane. In comparison with "bulk graphite" (0.34–0.35 nm), the d spacing value is higher due to oxygen-containing groups which may increase the interlayer distance or result in disoriented carbon atoms [23].

The morphological analysis of G-CDs is done by TEM, as illustrated in Fig. 2. The G-CDs are uniform-dispersed and exhibit a spherical particle shape (Fig. 2(a–d)). Fig. 2(e) reveals the size distribution diagram of G-CDs diameter, in which Gaussian fitting was performed, indicating that the average diameter of G-CDs is ~3.53 nm. Fig. 2(f) shows that the G-CDs have a uniform lattice distribution with the lattice spacing of 0.22 nm.

FT-IR spectroscopy ( Fig. 3) has extremely high structural sensitivity which is measured for identifying the functional groups with luminescent properties of the leaf-based G-CDs after surface passivation [24]. The broad peak located at approximately 2930 cm−1 is ascribed to the anti-symmetric stretching vibrations of -CH3 groups [25]. A vibration signal at 2565 cm−1 indicates the presence of P-OH groups in stretching vibration. The most intense signal at 1710 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of aldehyde -CO [26]. The peak at 1620 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of -CC- group. The peaks at 1430 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 are attributed to the bending vibration of -CH2 and -CH3 functional group, respectively [27]. The peaks at 1170 cm−1 and 1020 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of esters -C-O and amines -C-N functional group, respectively [28,29]. The peak located at about 860 cm−1 is ascribed to the out-of-plane bending vibration of -C-H groups. The vibration signals of oxygen-containing functional groups indicate that the as-prepared G-CDs have high water solubility.

The elemental presence and composition of the as-prepared G-CDs is analyzed by XPS ( Fig. 4). Fig. 4(a) is the XPS full-survey, which demonstrates that the G-CDs are primarily composed of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s elements, and the corresponding atomic content percentages are 55.97%, 41.82% and 2.21%, respectively. As the Fig. 4(b) shows, the C 1s core electrons spectrum with a central binding energy of 285.28 eV show five fitting peaks at 295.1 eV, 292.3 eV, 288.5 eV, 286.10 eV and 284.4 eV, which are imputed to the CC (6.19%), O-CO (12.16%), C-CO (26.90%), CO(24.56%), C-O/C-N(30.19%) groups. In Fig. 4(c), the binding energy at 531.97 eV is attributed to O 1s which can be deconvoluted into two fitted peaks at 531.79 eV and 533 eV, which are affiliated to CO (85.84%), and O-H/C-O (14.16%) groups, respectively [30]. In Fig. 4(d), the XPS spectrum of N 1s nuclear electrons locating at 400.21 eV can be ascribed to C-N.

The optical characteristics of the aqueous G-CDs are determined via UV–vis absorption, photoluminescence and fluorescence lifetime. Significantly, the calculated QY of G-CDs according to Eq. (1) is 18.3%, which is better compared to the CDs from other biomass sources in previous studies.

In Fig. 5(a), the UV–vis spectrum indicates that the G-CDs have two distinct optical absorption peaks at 325 nm and 350 nm with a tail extending into the visible region. The absorption peak at 325 nm corresponds to the π-π* transition of the sp2 carbons in the G-CDs, responsible for the trapping of excited-state energy by surface states [31]. An absorption peak is characteristic for the n-π* transition at 350 nm. As can be seen from the inset, the well-dispersed G-CDs aqueous is light brown under ambient light (left) and emits green bright fluorescence under 365 nm UV irradiation (right).

A wavelength-tunable emission is further explored (Fig. 5(b) and (c)). The photoluminescence spectra of G-CDs in 10 nm increments in the wavelength range of 400–500 nm have been investigated. Through testing, the wavelength at 420 nm is pinpointed as optimum excitation of G-CDs, which corresponds to the fluorescence emission wavelength at 505 nm (Fig. 5(b)). As displayed in Fig. 5(c), it is indicated that the G-CDs have excitation wavelength dependence, which exhibits a progressively red-shifting emission peak and the fluorescence intensity first increases and then decreases with the increase of excitation wavelength.

The fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs can be evaluated by decay curve (Fig. 5(d)) that abided by three-exponential fluorescence lifetime components: τ1 = 2.15 ns, τ2 = 0.4 ns and τ3 = 5.99 ns, and the average fluorescence lifetime obtained through fitting is τavg= 2.52 ns (χ2 =1.020944). The short lifetime indicates that the possible luminescent mechanism is the radiation recombination nature of excitations [32,33]. The radiative electron transitions primarily occur on the carbon cores with plenteous surface defects [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and sensitivity of p-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6(a–b), PL intensity of G-CDs could only be effectively quenched by only p-NP, indicating that G-CDs may be fluorescent sensor for p-NP detection. For sensitivity, G-CDs fluorescence quenching efficiency and p-NP concentration was studied. As shown in Fig. 6(c), the PL intensity of G-CDs decreases with increasing concentration of p-NP. Fig. 6(d) illustrates the linear relationship between PL intensity ratio of G-CDs and p-NP concentration (0–50 μM). The Lineweaver-Burk fitting equation is 1/(F0-F)= 0.000170/C(p-NP)+ 0.000282 (R2 =0.9951), and p-NP has a limit of detection (LOD) calculated at 0.0175 μM, which is the minimum detectable concentration of p-NP in water. In comparison to those of other p-NP determination methods, ours take advantage of highly sensitivity, low cost, and sustainability, as summarized in Table 1.

In order to further explore the practical applicability of G-CDs fluorescent sensor in the detection of p-NP in DI-water, tap water and lake water, standard recovery tests were carried out (Recovery=(p-NP found)/(p-NP added)× 100%). The results are shown in Table 2, it has a good consistency between the found and the added concentration of p-NP. It is determined that minerals and organic matter in water samples make little difference in p-NP determination as the recovery rates range 95.6% and 101.3%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is below 0.40%. In this way, the G-CDs as sensor have high accuracy and repeatability, which can effectively determine p-NP in actual water samples.

Generally, fluorescence quenching is divided into static quenching and dynamic quenching. The phenomenon that the quenching agent and the ground-state fluorescent molecules form non-luminescent ground-state complexes through weak binding is static quenching [41]. Dynamic quenching refers to that the excited fluorescent molecules collide with the quencher molecules to lose their excitation energy and return to the ground state by energy transfer mechanism [42]. Fig. 7(a) is the relative fluorescence intensity of G-CDs/p-NP reaction system at different temperatures, and the quenching degree decreases with the increase of temperature. Fig. 7(b) is the UV–vis absorption spectrum of G-CDs/p-NP reaction system after adding p-NP, the absorption decreased significantly, and the peak position shifted. Fig. 6(d) shows that the reaction between p-NP and G-CDs follows the Lineweaver-Burk equation. Fig. 7(c) shows that the fluorescence lifetime of G-CDs did not change after adding p-NP, indicating that there was no fluorescence resonance energy transfer between p-NP and G-CDs [43]. Fig. 7(d) shows that there is no overlap between the UV–vis absorption of p-NP and the excitation and emission spectra of G-CDs, thus excluding the existence of an internal filter effect [44]. Based on the above results, it was preliminarily inferred that the fluorescence fragmentation of G-CDs by p-NP was static quenching. The p-NP could strongly interact with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of electron-donating G-CDs, forming a bridging system of electron-donating and electronic-withdrawing. The mechanism of interplay between p-NP and G-CDs was presented in Fig. 8.

Fig. 9(a–c) is the plots: relative change in the concentration of the dyes with a function of time. It can be seen from the black lines in the figures, the degradation efficiencies of dyes alone emerge no significant change after sustainable sunlight irradiation, indicating that the three dyes have strong photo-stability, so the choice of MO, MG and MV dyes as catalytic models have research significance. Moreover, in dark environment, the degradation rates of the mixed solutions of G-CDs/dyes are less than 0.1. In sunlight irradiation environment, the degradation rates of MO, MG and MV dyes by G-CDs are ~98.0%, ~97.1% and ~63.6%under 40 min, 50 min and 90 min, respectively. To ensure the reliability of chemical catalysis, every experiment was performed three times to ensure even greater precision. Obviously, the G-CDs showed good stability in aqueous phase as a photocatalyst with no change in the degradation rate.

Fig. 9(d) shows that the ln(C0/C) values of G-CDs catalyst are linearly related to reaction time, exhibiting the degradation can well keep with pseudo first-order kinetic Eq. (2).
(2)
In
(
C
0
/ c
)
= k t
where C0 is the dye concentration after the 20 min equilibration with the photocatalyst before irradiation of sunlight, C is the concentration of dye models at reaction time t, and k is the apparent rate constant. R2 is the fitting coefficient, and close to 1, representing regular photodegradation behavior. The degradation rate constants k of MO, MG and MV dyes by G-CDs are 0.09876, 0.07716 and 0.01046 min−1.

To further explore the photocatalytic mechanism of G-CDs against MO, MG and MV dyes, the radical capture experiment was administrated by using IPA, PBQ and Na2-EDTA [45–47]. Obviously, for G-CDs photocatalyst, the degradation rate is evidently reduced with the presence of PBQ and IPA. Accordingly, it is clear that·OH and·O2 －take significant roles in the photocatalytic process. The addition of Na2-EDTA hardly affected the degradation efficiency of the G-CDs photocatalyst, indicating that H2O2 was not the main influencing factor for assessing each free radical's contribution. Fig. 10.

Based on the free radical trapping experiment, the possible mechanism of photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes by G-CDs is shown in Fig. 11. When photons with high-voltage energy radiate G-CDs, electrons transition from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), and holes are generated in the valence band. The charge carrier (electrons or holes) have strong reducing and oxidizing capabilities. The electrons react with O2 to form superoxide radical (·O2-), and the holes oxidize water to form hydroxyl radicals (·OH). Therefore,·O2- and·OH radicals adsorbed on the surface of G-CDs act as reactive oxygen species (ROS) to interact with dye molecules and photodegrade them into smaller molecules/fragments.

In summary, Cornus walteri leaves are converted into G-CDs (QY = 18.3%) via the combination of chemical oxidation and low-temperature hydrothermal treatment, which is a process of energy conservation. Typically, the G-CDs are 3.53 nm in average size, and its excitation and emission wavelengths are 420 nm and 505 nm, respectively. The obtained G-CDs can be used as a sensitive and selective fluorescent sensor to detect p-nitrophenol (p-NP), and the detection limit is as low as 0.0175 μM. At the same time, the practical detection of p-NP in actual water samples by G-CDs has also achieved satisfactory results. Moreover, under natural sunlight irradiation, the G-CDs exhibit a high photocatalytic efficiency in degrading MO, MG and MV dyes at low concentrations, which conforms to the pseudo first-order kinetics, and the degradation rate constants are 0.09876, 0.07716 and 0.01046 min−1. In this work, a green route to convert waste into a valuable product is provided, and dual-functional G-CDs are developed as fluorescent sensor and photocatalyst for monitoring and degradation of water environmental pollutants.

Congling Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jun Xu: Validation, Investigation. Ruili Zhang: Supervision, Project administration. Weilin Zhao: Resources, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant No. 51802114) and the Shandong Key Research and Development Plan (No. 2017GGX20127).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots with tailored surface functional groups for bioimaging of zebrafish, flocculation of heavy metal ions and ethanol detection
Appl. Surf. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.L. Liu, B.Q. Li, X.Y. Shi, L. Li, Y.J. Feng, D.C. Jia, Y. Zho, Target-oriented synthesis of high synthetic yield carbon dots with tailored surface functional groups for bioimaging of zebrafish, flocculation of heavy metal ions and ethanol detection, Appl. Surf. Sci., 538 (2021), pp. 148118

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrolysis assisted persulfate with annular iron sheet as anode for the enhanced degradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol in aqueous solution
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Li, Y. Ren, L. Lai, B. Lai, Electrolysis assisted persulfate with annular iron sheet as anode for the enhanced degradation of 2, 4-dinitrophenol in aqueous solution, J. Hazard. Mater., 344 (2018), pp. 778–787

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rhodococcus erythropolis as the receptor of cell based sensor for 2,4-dinitrophenol detection: effect of co-oxidation
Process Biochem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E.V. Emelyanova, A.N. Reshetilov, Rhodococcus erythropolis as the receptor of cell based sensor for 2,4-dinitrophenol detection: effect of co-oxidation, Process Biochem., 37 (2002), pp. 683–692
